questions.question: Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Explain why the relationship between them is important for multithreaded applications.
keyPoints: User-level threads: managed in user space, fast, portable, kernel unaware
Kernel-level threads: managed by OS kernel, slower but OS can schedule
Relationship determines concurrency, blocking behavior, multiprocessor support
Model selection impacts performance and complexity

questions.question: Describe the Many-to-One multithreading model with a diagram. What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: Multiple user threads mapped to single kernel thread
Thread management done in user space by thread library
Advantage: efficient, portable, no kernel overhead for thread management
Disadvantage: if one thread blocks (system call), all threads block, no multiprocessor support

questions.question: Explain the One-to-One multithreading model. How does it overcome the limitations of the Many-to-One model?
keyPoints: Each user thread maps to one kernel thread
Allows true parallel execution on multiprocessors
Better concurrency than Many-to-One
Disadvantage: overhead of creating kernel threads limits number of concurrent threads

questions.question: Compare FCFS and Round Robin scheduling algorithms with respect to response time and waiting time.
keyPoints: FCFS: simple, non-preemptive, poor response time for short jobs, convoy effect
Round Robin: time-sliced preemptive, good response time for interactive systems
FCFS has variable waiting time depending on arrival order
RR provides more uniform waiting time but may increase context switching overhead

questions.question: What is the Many-to-Many model? How does it balance the advantages of both Many-to-One and One-to-One models?
keyPoints: Multiplexes many user threads to fewer kernel threads
User threads can be scheduled on available kernel threads
Achieves true concurrency without overhead of One-to-One
Solves blocking problem of Many-to-One and thread count limitation of One-to-One

questions.question: Define thread scheduling criteria. Explain any three criteria with examples.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: percentage of time CPU is busy
Throughput: number of processes completed per unit time
Turnaround Time: time from process submission to completion
Waiting Time: total time process spends in ready queue
Response Time: time from submission to first response

questions.question: What is priority scheduling? Explain the problem of starvation and how aging addresses it.
keyPoints: Priority scheduling: higher priority processes execute first
Starvation: low-priority processes may never get CPU if high-priority processes keep arriving
Aging: gradually increase priority of waiting processes over time
This ensures even low-priority processes eventually get executed

questions.question: Explain all the major multithreading models (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many, Two-level) with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Many-to-One: multiple user to single kernel, efficient but blocks all if one blocks
One-to-One: one-to-one mapping, true parallelism but overhead of many kernel threads
Many-to-Many: multiplexed mapping, balance of efficiency and concurrency
Two-level: hybrid approach, some bound threads like One-to-One, others multiplexed like Many-to-Many
Comparison table covering: multiprocessor support, blocking behavior, overhead, complexity

questions.question: Describe the SJF scheduling algorithm. Explain why it provides optimal average waiting time. Discuss the challenges in implementing SJF and the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive SJF.
keyPoints: SJF selects process with shortest burst time
Mathematically optimal for average waiting time proof
Challenge: knowing burst times in advance (not always possible)
Non-preemptive SJF: once started, runs to completion
Preemptive SJF (SRTF): if new shorter process arrives, preempt current
Example showing optimality with sample burst times

questions.question: Explain the concept of multiprocessor scheduling. Discuss load balancing, processor affinity, and the challenges of scheduling in symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) systems.
keyPoints: Multiprocessor scheduling assigns threads to multiple CPUs
Load balancing: distribute work evenly across processors
Processor affinity: keep thread on same processor for cache efficiency (soft/hard affinity)
SMP: all processors equal, can run any thread
Challenges: cache coherence, migration overhead, synchronization
Approaches: dedicated processor assignment, load sharing, gang scheduling

questions.question: Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Discuss Round Robin scheduling in detail with a suitable example showing how turnaround time and waiting time are calculated.
keyPoints: Preemptive: OS can forcibly suspend running process (Round Robin, SRTF, Priority)
Non-preemptive: process runs until blocks or completes (FCFS, SJF)
Round Robin: time quantum q, processes in circular queue
Example: 3 processes with burst times and quantum calculation
Context switches increase with smaller quantum
Good for time-sharing, fair but may have high overhead
